title: Nurse Rostering (3 nurses, 2 days, 2 shifts)
summary: Assign nurses to shifts such that staffing requirements and per-nurse limits are respected.
problem_statement: I have 3 nurses (A, B, C) and 2 days (Mon, Tue). Each day has 2 shifts: day-shift and night-shift. Each shift needs exactly 1 nurse. No nurse may work both a night-shift and the following day's day-shift (no back-to-back). No nurse may work more than 2 shifts total across the 2 days. Find a valid roster.
real_world_example: A small clinic balancing nurse availability against minimum-coverage and labor-law rules. Real-world rostering scales to dozens of staff, weeks of days, and seven or eight shift types, but the constraint shape is the same.

expected_pattern.variables: x_{n,d,s} in {0, 1} — whether nurse n is on shift s of day d (3 × 2 × 2 = 12 binaries)
expected_pattern.objective: feasibility (objective = 0); soft preferences can be added as positive weights on bad assignments
expected_pattern.constraints: each (day, shift): exactly one nurse; per-nurse total shifts <= 2; no back-to-back: x_{n,d,night} + x_{n,d+1,day} <= 1

expected_solution_summary: One valid roster: Mon-day = A, Mon-night = B, Tue-day = C, Tue-night = A. Each nurse works ≤2 shifts; no back-to-back violations. Multiple valid rosters exist.
learning_notes: Nurse rostering is where 'hard vs soft constraints' first matters in practice. Phase 5B treats them all as hard (objective = 0) so any feasible roster is optimal; real deployments add a positive weight per preference violation to the objective and minimize the weighted sum. The constraint families generalize to crew scheduling, classroom assignment, and tournament scheduling — same shape, different domain names.
